data.title: Antero Resources Now Decisively, Financially In The Black (Upgrade)
data.description:
Antero Resources (NYSE:AR) reported stronger quarterly cash flows than during the past few years. The company repurchased stock and paid down a significant portion of its debt. This reflects a very positive outlook for its business going forward. When considering comments in the previous call, management stated its priorities for cash flows placed debt repayment ahead of every other opportunity. Yet, they did both, repurchased stock and retired debt. This shows their strong positive posture, with still $300 million left in debt to extinguish. The income sheet turned black. This continues our coverage of Resources. Our last writing, discussed trading with natural gas demand cycles. Now, well, yes, it is hard to see things in the black, but would you humor us and come look. Maybe, just maybe, we can squint really hard and see something.

Quarter Results

From the 1st quarter presentation, we include several slides. The 1st shows the cash flow.

The second and third show debt.

Although there is no short-term debt payments required, the next slide shows the amount left on the credit facility now at $300 million, approximately one quarter in cash flow.

Other important results included:

  • High premium vs. Mont Belvieu year over year with C3+ NGL prices pegged at up to $2.50.
    • To the extent that the liquids portion pricing trends with crude oil, and in the past, it has, weaker crude prices will be a headwind.
  • Production of natural gas remained constant even with lower capital costs.

The company is now in very good financial shape. In a few more quarters, the last portion of debt, the $300 million plus a lot more shares of stock, will likely be gone.

Collar Hedge

The next announcement from management was an interesting approach for this group. They announced a collar for hedging future natural gas production beginning in 2026. A slide included next summarizes the agreement.

This collar approach helps take advantage of what is left of the untapped wells, especially in the Haynesville region.

Also, we wrote in Evaluating Predicted Stair Stepped Natural Gas Spot Prices And More about the natural progression of natural gas pricing. We wrote:

We continue writing on NG with our last writing, Natural Gas' Hidden Future Of Muted Rising Prices, highlighting the existence of spare capacity, wells drilled, once in use, now idled during the recent production glut. We predicted prices would follow a stair step approach in their return to higher values for the next few years. Guessing the actual height of each stair is and was a dubious task with many unknowns."

Next, a slide from Antero Resources helps understand the range.

For Haynesville to really kick in, the traditional swing region, the price must be above $3.5.

The financial effect guarantees at least $100 million cash at the low end and as much as $400 million max. The collar will likely pay for approximately half of next year's capital spending. The spending for 2025 is guided at approximately $600 million.

Marketplace

Continuing with the marketplace discussion, a very good back and forth between Neil Mehta of Goldman Sachs, and Michael Kennedy, company CFO, discussed Resources view:

[W]e think Haynesville is probably $4 plus, and that's probably the inventory right now, and that runs out pretty quick. So I mean, no one really talks about inventory fatigue, but you'll definitely see it across the basin. So all this gas supply is going to have to come from not your Appalachian low-cost producers. It's going to come from ever-increasing second-tier acreage and second-tier basin. So that price will continue to go up.

Management, also, included a slide illustrating the value of being in the very prime of prime locations. A mother-lode worth of new demand is locating right smack dap in the middle of or adjacent to Resources' properties.

With the data center build out, the close proximity for relatively inexpensive energy bodes extremely positive for future markets.

Regarding Resources bullish stance on the Marcellus and other northern regions, MPLX (MPLX) management stated:

However, we continue to see robust production across our Marcellus, Utica and Permian operating regions. These basins have some of the lowest breakeven prices in the U.S., offering economically advantaged and development opportunities. Based on feedback from our producer customers, we continue to expect year-over-year volume growth."

Antero management added their opinion:

The extensive resource base of the Marcellus and Utica Shales provides certainty of long-term natural gas supply while supportive state regulations are leading to fast approvals and attractive incentives to build in the region."

With premier resources, the company sits in the investor's catbird's seat.

Risk & Reward

Antero Resources is an extremely well-run company positioned in the best of the best natural gas resources inside the U.S. It processes little debt with a leverage of 1.1 and heading lower. The free cash flow for 2025 was estimated at approximately $1 billion at the end of 2024 report. Into the future, growth in market size looms positive. When asked about stock repurchases verses debt repayment, Kennedy offered this:

Yes, John, yes, we're opportunistic and we're trying to be countercyclical. We didn't think we'd get this opportunity to buy shares at these prices. I mean, when you look at our fundamental outlook, it's as strong as ever. And to have the shares valued at where it is today is an opportunity for us. So that's why we pivoted towards share buybacks. We have a lot of visibility into our business and our cash flow generation and we don't really have any debt, so very little debt."

Management believes that the stock is dirt cheap.

What seems most interesting for us and maybe for other long-term investors is what happens when the company exhausts all of the debt and most of the stock? Do they buy something with the massive cash flows? Do they pay huge dividends once again? Or will they balance the two? The business will generate enough cash to buy almost all the debt this year, that is if they choose. Over the next some years, at $40 per share and $2 billion a year in cash flows, they could purchase all of the stock. When is enough, enough? Do they purchase half or two-thirds? Or?

The important driving force for investing seems related mostly to price and seasonal swings. We have traded the company twice in the past few months resulting in nice profits but are sitting on the sidelines for now. If the price drops lower and offers a technical entrance point, we plan to rebuy. Our gut feeling suggests that the summer season is close enough for a long cycle entrance. For those with long-term investing intentions, the recent price in the high 30s probability represents a good entrance. We rate this a buy with some level of caution that lower prices might appear.

The major risk, in our view, is once again too much production. Recent EIA storage and production reports show that storage is at or slightly below the 5-year average, and that production is slowly slowing down. These two parameters are by far the most important to watch. Management expects Haynesville production to continue acting as the swing region, with at least $4 spot price to maintain that function. This dampens risk.

Now what did you see in the black? Actually, we saw a lot, a lot of very positive indicators. We certainly hope you did.

data.title: eGain Q3: Slower AI Knowledge Management Growth, But Outlook Points To Strong Rebound
data.description:
Investment thesis

eGain's (EGAN) Q3 results showed the company falling short of revenue guidance, yet beating on earnings expectations. When it comes to my bullish thesis for the stock, in my previous write-up I had stated:

If there are signs that AI knowledge management is not gaining traction in what is a highly competitive market, that would severely dent my bullish thesis.

When I look at how the AI knowledge management segment performed during this quarter, it is clear that year over year ARR growth of 11% was much weaker than expected. Despite finding this concerning, I do, however, see other positives, including management's expectation for growth to return to the high teens for the next quarter. With the company continuing to maintain solid profitability and shares trading at just 2.5 times AI knowledge management revenue, I still find the current valuation compelling enough to maintain my Buy rating.

Soft quarter, but the outlook remains promising

Despite outperforming with respect to earnings, management lowered its outlook for full-year revenue. While much of this was attributed to lower margin professional services revenue, there was also an element of weakness associated with AI knowledge management SaaS ARR which saw its year-over-year growth rate decelerate to 11%, compared to 17% in the prior quarter. Given that AI knowledge management revenue, which represents around half of eGain's total revenue, forms the crux of my investment thesis, I need to dig into the reason for this weakness and how it may play out going forward.

Though management did not clearly state the reason for this sharp deceleration, a closer look at the net retention rates show that there was either higher churn or reduced usage by existing customers, as this metric dropped from 99% in Q2 to 97% in Q3. While I acknowledge that this is indeed concerning, what outweighs it is the fact that management is guiding for growth to return to the high teens next quarter. A crucial factor underpinning this is the expansion with a top four US bank, likely JPMorgan Chase (JPM), a case study which is described in detail on eGain's website. Expanding with such as significant customer not only helps due to the higher revenue contribution, but also proves that the company's solution is finding good traction in a competitive market.

On the whole, while the sharp deceleration in growth this quarter dented my bull case, I believe that the improved outlook and the signing of a major client of this magnitude, which follows the signing of one of the largest global airlines in Q2, keeps the bull case very much intact. The company is set to benefit from strong industry tailwinds with forecasts calling for a 43.7% CAGR for AI knowledge management systems until 2034. Additionally, headwinds from the loss of two large legacy business customers will diminish going forward as they have now been lapped, which should support a return to topline growth for total revenue.

Balancing valuation with the risks I see

Turning to valuation, I continue to assign no value to eGain's stagnant conversation and analytics segments, and instead solely focus on its AI knowledge management business. Based on AI knowledge management revenue for FY25 of approximately $43 million, shares are currently valued at a revenue multiple of 3.4. The company has a net cash position after deducting its current liabilities of close to $40 million, which when factored into its valuation, brings the revenue multiple down to just 2.5.

I find this valuation to be highly attractive given that growth rates are expected to accelerate to the high teens, while the company continues to remain solidly profitable despite its ongoing growth investments. In comparison, a peer like NICE (NICE) is valued at nearly 3.5 times revenue, while posting mid-single digit growth rates.

Despite the accelerating growth and relatively cheap valuation of EGAN stock, investors should closely track its retention levels in the coming quarters, to ensure that churn is not increasing. Moreover, the AI knowledge management space remains highly competitive, especially with some players like Verint Systems (VRNT) integrating their solutions together with their contact center offerings.

While I chose not to delve deeper into the conversation and analytics segments, their revenue and earnings have remained stable, and investors need this stable performance to continue in order for management to fund the growth initiatives related to AI knowledge management.

Conclusion

eGain's Q3 earnings brought about a mixed picture where AI knowledge management ARR decelerated sharply, yet management expects a sharp rebound next quarter, helped by a significant customer win. The current valuation remains appealing given the promising growth outlook, which makes me stick with my Buy rating for the stock.

data.title: EM Bond Performance Remains Solid In 2025
data.description:
The VanEck Emerging Markets Bond Fund was up 1.39% in April, compared to up 1.51% for its benchmark. Year-to-date, the fund is up 5.94%, compared to 4.84% for its benchmark. During April, China led outperformers; the team continues to see the worst of the Chinese property crisis as behind us. Underweights in Czechia and Malaysia led underperformers. We remain cautious on US dollar (USD) duration, USD spread duration, but open on local-currency duration. The fund has around 60% in curated local currency, 40% in USD bonds, with a noteworthy underweight to Brazil and South Africa in local currency. Carry is 8.2%, YTW is 9.9%, and duration is 5.3.

Last month we attended the IMF Spring Meetings. Read our key takeaways here.

Average Annual Total Returns* (%) (In USD)

As of April 30, 2025
1 Mo 3 Mo YTD 1 Yr 3 Yrs 5 Yrs 10 Yrs
Class A: NAV (Inception 07/09/12) 1.24 2.39 4.69 9.20 6.42 7.11 2.34
Class A: Maximum 5.75% load -4.58 -3.50 -1.33 2.92 4.34 5.85 1.73
Class I: NAV (Inception 07/09/12) 1.28 2.48 4.63 9.51 6.75 7.45 2.64
Class Y: NAV (Inception 07/09/12) 1.23 2.44 4.76 9.44 6.65 7.36 2.57
50% GBI-EM/50% EMBI 1.51 3.04 4.84 9.31 5.71 2.62 2.19

* Returns less than one year are not annualized.

Expenses: Class A: Gross 1.83%, Net 1.21%; Class I: Gross 1.37%, Net 0.86%; Class Y: Gross 1.33%, Net 0.96%. Expenses are capped contractually until 05/01/26 at 1.20% for Class A, 0.85% for Class I, 0.95% for Class Y. Caps exclude acquired fund fees and expenses, interest, trading, dividends, and interest payments of securities sold short, taxes, and extraordinary expenses.

The performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Performance may be lower or higher than performance data quoted. Please call 800.826.2333 or visit vaneck.com for performance current to the most recent month ended.

The “Net Asset Value” (NAV) of a Fund is determined at the close of each business day, and represents the dollar value of one share of the fund; it is calculated by taking the total assets of the fund, subtracting total liabilities, and dividing by the total number of shares outstanding. The NAV is not necessarily the same as the ETF’s intraday trading value. Investors should not expect to buy or sell shares at NAV.

Exposure Types and Significant Changes

The changes to our top positions are summarized below. Our largest positions in April were Mexico, Brazil, Thailand, South Africa, and Poland.

  • We increased our local currency exposure in Brazil, Thailand, and South Africa. Brazil shows signs of tentative fiscal improvement, its direct trade exposure to the U.S. is limited, inflation expectations had peaked, bringing us closer to the end of the tightening cycle. These factors improved the policy and technical test scores for the country. Thailand is a higher-yielding beneficiary of China’s restrained approach to the trade war, which improves its technical test score. South Africa’s budget debates are not over yet, but the main coalition partners confirmed that the government of national unity remains in place, improving the policy test score for the country.
  • We also increased our hard currency sovereign exposure in Ecuador, Argentina, and Nigeria. In Ecuador, the incumbent’s victory in the presidential elections should strengthen the reform mandate and the country’s policy test score. Argentina just got a new IMF program, which was conditional on a major overhaul of the country’s exchange rate regime with a freer floating currency and no FX intervention within the currency band. This boosted Argentina’s policy test score. We continue to see more green shoots in the Nigerian economy, and 80% of Nigeria’s exports is oil, which is exempt from the tariffs, which strengthens the economic and technical test scores for the country.
  • Finally, we increased our hard currency sovereign exposure in Gabon, Senegal, and Pakistan. In Gabon, we now have more certainty on the policy front after the former transitional president Nguema comfortably won the presidential election, improving the policy test score for the country.

    In Senegal, the IMF signaled that it is ready to move on a new program after the country addressed some data misreporting issues. This should strengthen the policy test score for Senegal. Pakistan’s valuations look better now, and the country’s direct exposure to the U.S. tariffs is limited. The U.S. is expected to keep Pakistan in its orbit for political reasons, and the IMF funding is expected to get unlocked after the board’s approval. In terms of our investment process, this improved the policy and technical test scores for the country.

  • We reduced our local currency exposure in Indonesia, Chile, Mexico, and Turkey. The Indonesian government has a communication problem, especially as regards its fiscal plans against the backdrop of downside growth risks. The central bank’s focus on currency stability may limit room for rate cuts. In terms of our investment process, this weakens the policy test score for the country. The softening growth outlook in Chile’s main trade partners and its potential impact on demand for copper worsen the economic and technical test scores for the country. Mexico’s growth prospects can be also adversely affected by a rising risk of recession in the U.S. Turkey might be well positioned to deal with the trade war challenges, but its domestic political scene remains noisy, worsening the policy test score for the country.

  • We also reduced our hard currency corporate exposure in China, Hong Kong, and Singapore. The main reasons are the negative and more direct impact of higher tariffs, and the non-zero risk of the renminbi’s devaluation to regain competitiveness.
  • Finally, we reduced our hard currency sovereign exposure in Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Bahamas, the Philippines, Cote d'Ivoire, and Papua New Guinea. The key concern in Saudi Arabia, Bahrain and the Philippines was the impact of the U.S. tariffs on inflation expectations and duration. The tariffs’ negative impact on lower-income fragile economies was the main consideration in Cote d'Ivoire and Papua New Guinea. In terms of our investment process, this has worsened the technical test score for these countries.

Disclosures

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the author(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

Duration measures a bond's sensitivity to interest rate changes that reflects the change in a bond's price given a change in yield. This duration measure is appropriate for bonds with embedded options. Carry is the benefit or cost for owning an asset. Yield to worst is a measure of the lowest possible yield that can be received on a bond with an early retirement provision. Averages are market weighted. The yields presented do not represent the performance of the Fund. These statistics do not take into account fees and expenses associated with investments of the Fund.

All indices are unmanaged and include the reinvestment of all dividends, but do not reflect the payment of transaction costs, advisory fees or expenses that are associated with an investment in the Fund. Certain indices may take into account withholding taxes. An index's performance is not illustrative of the Fund's performance. Indices are not securities in which investments can be made.

The Fund's benchmark index (50% GBI-EM/50% EMBI) is a blended index consisting of 50% J.P. Morgan Government Bond Index-Emerging Markets (GBI-EM) Global Diversified and 50% J.P. Morgan Emerging Markets Bond Index (EMBI). The J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Diversified tracks local currency bonds issued by Emerging Markets governments. The J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Diversified tracks returns for actively traded external debt instruments in emerging markets, and is also J.P. Morgan's most liquid U.S. dollar emerging markets debt benchmark.

The Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index measures the performance of global investment grade fixed income securities.

The FTSE Treasury Benchmark 10 year measures the return of the 10 year U.S. Treasury.

ICE BofA Global Broad Market Index tracks the performance of investment grade debt publicly issued in the major domestic and eurobond markets, including sovereign, quasi-government, corporate, securitized and collateralized securities. ICE BofA Current 10-Year U.S. Treasury Index is comprised of the most recently issued 10-year U.S. Treasury note.

The MSCI ACWI Index is a global equity benchmark that captures large- and mid-cap stocks across 23 developed and 24 emerging markets, representing approximately 85% of the global investable equity universe.

The S&P 500 Index is a widely recognized U.S. equity benchmark that tracks 500 of the largest publicly traded companies, reflecting the performance of the core U.S. stock market.

Information has been obtained from sources believed to be reliable but J.P. Morgan does not warrant its completeness or accuracy. The Index is used with permission. The index may not be copied, used or distributed without J.P. Morgan's written approval. Copyright 2025, J.P. Morgan Chase & Co. All rights reserved.

You can lose money by investing in the Fund. Any investment in the Fund should be part of an overall investment program, not a complete program. The Fund is subject to risks which may include, but are not limited to, risks associated with active management, credit, credit-linked notes, currency management strategies, derivatives, emerging market issuers, energy sector, ESG investing strategy, foreign currency, foreign securities, hedging, high portfolio turnover, high yield securities, interest rate, market, non-diversified, operational, restricted securities, investing in other funds, sovereign bond, and special risks considerations of investing in African, Asian and Latin American issuers, all of which may adversely affect the Fund. Emerging market issuers and foreign securities may be subject to securities markets, political and economic, investment and repatriation restrictions, different rules and regulations, less publicly available financial information, foreign currency and exchange rates, operational and settlement, and corporate and securities laws risks. Derivatives may involve certain costs and risks such as liquidity, interest rate, and the risk that a position could not be closed when most advantageous.

Investing involves substantial risk and high volatility, including possible loss of principal. An investor should consider the investment objective, risks, charges and expenses of a Fund carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus and summary prospectus, which contain this and other information, call 800.826.2333 or visit vaneck.com. Please read the prospectus and summary prospectus carefully before investing.

No part of this material may be reproduced in any form, or referred to in any other publication, without express written permission of Van Eck Securities Corporation.

© 2025 Van Eck Securities Corporation, Distributor, a wholly-owned subsidiary of Van Eck Associates Corporation.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Top Merger Stocks Held By Fund Managers - Mid-Q2 2025
data.description:
The tariff news in the last month and a half has affected every slice of the global markets. So M&A has not been spared. With the uncertainty surrounding tariffs, newly announced mergers ground to a halt. (This past week, with at least a temporary calm hovering over the market, a fair number of deals have been announced). And with the risk-free rate of return at 4.5% or so, many arb funds have elected to hold more in short-term treasuries and less in merger stocks.

But rain or shine, each quarter, I compile the top 10 stocks held in the funds that specialize in merger stocks and share it with our Seeking Alpha community.

My rules:

  • I only count a stock if it is at least 1% of the fund's portfolio.
  • I only include stocks that are still actively trading.
  • I only choose funds where the majority of positions are merger-related.

The top stock for the second straight quarter is Kellanova (K). 21 of my 32 funds have positions in Kellanova. Almost a year ago, candy giant Mars Incorporated agreed to buy Kellanova for almost $36 Billion including assumed net leverage which amounts to $83.50 per share. The proposed merger would unite Mars, a global leader in pet care, snacking, and food, with Kellanova, a company known for its global snacking, international cereal and noodles. The deal is expected to close in this quarter. Approvals from the United States, and the European Commission are still needed. Including Kellanova's 2.8% dividend, and assuming a late June close, the deal offers approximately a 19% IRR.

Hess Corporation (HES) is second, appearing in 13 funds. If it feels like this deal has been on the books forever, well, it has. Back in October 2023, Chevron (CVX) agreed to buy Hess in an all-stock deal. If the deal closes, Hess shareholders will receive 1.025 shares of Chevron for each Hess share. What has held up the merger has been a hearing over Exxon Mobil's (XOM) dispute over Hess's Guyana assets. That hearing will occur in less than two weeks, on May 26. It is expected that an arbitration panel will make a decision by late August or September. The IRR is a respectable 22% for a stock that may not have much if any downside should the merger not be completed.

Top 10 Merger Arb Stocks Held By Funds

1) Kellanova Held by 21 funds 19% IRR
2) Hess Held by 17 funds 22% IRR
3) Frontier Communications Parent (FYBR) Held by 15 funds 8% IRR
4) Discover Financial Services (DFS) Held by 14 funds 10% IRR
5) H&E Equipment Services (HEES) Held by 14 funds 13% IRR
6) ANSYS, Inc. (ANSS) Held by 13 funds *
7) Juniper Networks (JNPR) Held by 12 funds 62% IRR
8) Spirit AeroSystems Holdings (SPR) Held by 10 funds 2% IRR
9) Amedisys (AMED) Held by 9 funds 66% IRR
10) United States Steel Corporation (X) Held by 9 funds *

*Short-term event-driven dates

When a fund makes an M&A stock its top holding, it signals strong conviction that the deal will ultimately close. Ten funds had Hess as its top pick.

Top Positions Among The 32 Funds

Hess Top Position in 10 Funds
Kellanova Top Position in 3 Funds

Amedisys

 Top Position in 2 Funds

Some arb funds oversize the positions that they have great conviction in. As of the end of quarter one, there were a good number of stocks that were at least 11% of a fund.

Kellanova 29% of a fund
Hess 29% of a fund

Juniper Networks

 24% of a fund

Frontier Communications Parent

 23% of a fund
Amedisys 15% of a fund
United States Steel Corporation 14% of a fund
Spirit AeroSystems Holdings 11% of a fund

Conclusion

While there is no substitute for doing one's own work and developing one's unique trading/investing style, looking at what some of the top arb hedge funds are doing with their money is quite valuable. These funds have more resources, staff, and contacts than an individual investor can possibly have.

While there is no guarantee that the above deals will close, the fact is, historically, approximately 94% of all mergers do close and when the M&A professionals allocate their money to an arb position the odds even get better.

data.title: ZIM Integrated Shipping Has Potential To Benefit From Trade Wars
data.description:
Business of ZIM Integrated Shipping Services

ZIM Integrated Shipping Services (NYSE:ZIM) is one of the world's largest container shipping liners. By the end of 2024, its fleet consisted of 143 ships, including 15 vehicle carriers and 128 container ships with a capacity of 1,000 to 15,000 TEU, or twenty foot equivalent units, which are standard shipping containers. The combined capacity of the fleet totaled 784,800 TEU, or approximately 6,000 TEU per ship.

About 90% of ZIM's revenue is derived from the transportation of containers and vehicles, with the remaining 10% coming from ancillary services, such as shipment tracking or escorting special cargos.

ZIM operates across almost all key trade routes, including:

  • Trans-Pacific shipments (Asia – America, 43%)
  • Trans-Atlantic shipments (Europe – America – Africa, 15%)
  • The Suez Canal канал (mostly Asia – Europe, 9%)
  • Transasia (20%)
  • Americas (South America – North America, 13%)

The investment thesis

The idea about ZIM rests on several key factors:

    • Rising freight rates in the container shipping segment. The market has relatively recently recovered from the consequences of the crisis in the Red Sea and geopolitical escalation in the Middle East, when rates fell by more than 60% from local highs. Now the tariffs imposed by the Trump administration represent a new shock to the economy. We expect their introduction to disrupt global supply chains and snarl trade routes, making transportation more expensive. For example, according to FBX (Freight Rate Global Container Index) in the six months since March 2018, when Trump imposed duties on steel and aluminum imports from some countries, freight rates rose from $1,200-$1,300 per FEU to $1,700, or by 30%-40%. The magnitude of the current protectionist measures is much more serious, as they affect almost all imports from most countries of the world.

  • High exposure to spot-market rates. Many carriers try to increase the share of contract-based income in the revenue structure to even out market volatility. ZIM management, on the contrary, said it is increasing the share of spot transportation. In 2024, 65% of shipments in the Pacific region were spot-market-based. On the one hand, in the long run, this makes for more volatile results that are hard to predict. On the other hand, potential profits are higher whenever market conditions are favorable.
  • Flexibility. ZIM operates under the “asset-light” model: More than 90% of vessels are chartered in (leased). However, the company has options to not renew the charters for some of the fleet. So we estimate, in 2025 and 2026, ZIM can return up to 20%-22% of its total capacity if necessary (for example, if market conditions deteriorate). In an unfavorable environment, this could pare losses.
  • Opportunity to ramp up shipping volumes. In 2024, according to data from the 10-K filing, 89%-90% of the entire ZIM's capacity was chartered (utilization peaked at 99% in 2020 and 2021). This means that, if demand goes up, the company has the capacity to ramp up volumes. In addition, we calculated that the average quarterly vessel utilization rate (which is average loaded volume in terms of TEU divided by total capacity) has fallen from 1.56x before the Red Sea crisis to 1.25x. That means one ship now makes 20% fewer voyages on average. We believe the vessel utilization rate could recover in 2026 along with a possible “reopening” of the Red Sea.

Outlook for ZIM's financial results

Below are several assumptions that we make in our forecasts:

1) In 2024, the company completed a fleet transformation that lasted more than 2 years, increasing the number of vessels and raising the share of higher-capacity container ships. We believe management to maintain current capacity levels, adjusting them when necessary, depending on market conditions. In 2025, ZIM's ~115,000 TEU charter will expire (relative to early 2024) and ~108,000 TEUs are available for renewal. In the base case scenario, we expect all charters available for renewal to be renewed.

2) In early April, FBX freight rates fell to their lowest since 2023, to $2,050/FEU. In our moderately optimistic scenario, we anticipate rates to stabilize at current levels - they have been stable for 5 weeks - and then rise about 35% by the end of 2025 (just like during Trump's 1st term). However, we assume that in the event that current supply chains get disrupted, trade routes could become more complicated, which would cause freight rates to rise to the local highs of 2024 ($5000-5500) or even higher.

3) Average vessel utilization rate will hold at 2024 levels in 2025. The “reopening” of the Red Sea is not yet viewed as a base-case scenario, nor is a significant easing of geopolitical tensions. However, we expect the utilization rate to rise to 2022–2023 levels as soon as 2026.

Vessel utilization = (total TEU shipped / total vessels)

average capacity = (total capacity / total vessels)

We base our valuation on the moderately optimistic scenario. According to this scenario, revenue will total $7.7 bln (-9% y/y) in 2025, and $9.9 bln (+29% y/y due to recovery in ship utilization) in 2026. The revenue is supposed to be lower in 2025 because we estimate that average freight rates will be lower in comparison to the previous year too (but higher than current levels).

Under this scenario, EBITDA is projected at $2.9 bln (-20% y/y) in 2025, and $4 bln (+36% y/y) in 2026.

ZIM's FCFE and dividends

ZIM's dividend policy provides for quarterly payments of ~30% of net income for 1–3 quarters and 30%-50% for the full year. The company may also announce special dividends in special market conditions (for example, this was the case in 2022 when freight rates were record high). We expect that in 2025 and 2026 the company will pay 30% of net income for 1–3 quarters and 70% for 4Q (the arrangement was the same for 2024), so the total payout for the year will be 42%.

Risks that can shatter the investment case

  • Low predictability of financial performance. ZIM has significant exposure to the spot market of container shipping, which has been extremely volatile over the last 5 years. A 30%-40% increase or decrease in rates within a single year is normal practice for the industry in the current environment. Consequently, ZIM’s earnings are also subject to significant fluctuations in the event of a sharp decline in freight rates.
  • High sensitivity to global affairs. Maritime trade is the lifeblood of the modern economy. Geopolitical and macroeconomic events, such as recession in the US and the world, changes in tariff policy, or new armed conflicts, can significantly affect freight rates and, by extension, ZIM’s earnings.

Valuation

Depending on the dynamics of freight rates, there are several scenarios for 2025.

-Conservative scenario: Trade wars die down, freight costs stabilize at current levels ($2,050/FEU) and then rise along with inflation. Target share price is $13 using 2,6x target EV / EBITDA multiple, which we believe to be fair. FWD FCF yield is (-8%). The potential dividend yield is 15% of the current price.

-Moderately optimistic scenario: Freight rates rise by ~35% from current levels, as they did when tariffs were imposed during Trump's first term. The target share price is $27. FWD FCF yield is 16%. The potential dividend yield is 27.3%.

-Optimistic scenario: Rates recover to the local high of 2024 ($5,500/FEU or x2.7 to the current level). The target price is $33. FWD FCF yield is 94%. The dividend yield is 67%.

Conclusion

ZIM has a volatile investment case. Its high exposure to spot-market freight rates — while introducing significant variability — also positions it to benefit disproportionately in periods of rate expansion. The asset-light model and chartering flexibility offers a defensive cushion in the event of downturns, while current vessel utilization levels still offer room to scale up as trade volumes recover.

Although we project a decline in revenue and EBITDA in 2025, this is primarily due to the residual effect of lower average freight rates early in the year. However, rising protectionist trends and ongoing disruptions in global shipping lanes support the thesis that freight rates are more likely to rise than fall in the medium term. If rates follow a trajectory similar to that observed after the 2018 tariff wave, ZIM is well positioned to benefit.

data.title: Genco Shipping: Tailwinds Are Fading, Time To Hold Steady
data.description:
Overview

Genco Shipping & Trading (GNK) is a US-based dry bulk shipping company focused on global commodity transport. The figure below summarises the different types of bulk that is carried by vessels. The advantage present for dry bulk is the longest port time as well. Moreover, the figure also explains how 40% of global tonnage is transferred in the form of dry bulk.

It operates a set of vessels consisting of Capesize, Panamax and Ultramax/Supramax dry cargo vessels. The illustration is given below about where these ships fall in the spectrum of the different types of dry bulk ships based on the deadweight tonnage [DWT]. DWT is the indication of the weight that a ship can safely carry which includes all the weight except for the weight of the ship itself.

However, we also need to take a look at how well Genco Shipping is utilising the fleet that it currently has in service. The data below provides us with an insight as well about what is the fleet composition, what is their rate of utilisation and what is the operating expense. The insight that we can clearly gain is that Genco’s utilisation is relatively lesser for their larger vessels compared to the smaller vessels. This can potentially prove to be a problem given that the cost of operating the relatively larger vessel – Capesize – is higher vis-a-vis the smaller vessels like Panamax, Supramax, etc. This will be explored further in the subsequent sections.

The company operates on the basis of a mix of spot and time-charter agreements. The spot agreements are short-term and one-voyage agreements, whereas the time-charter agreements are longer-term leases that are agreed based on a fixed daily rate of payment. The upside of the spot agreement is that during positive market movement, Genco will be able to take advantage of market upsides by charging prices at the market rate. However, herein lies the unpredictability, which leads to volatility in cash flows. On the other hand, the time-charter agreement is likely to provide higher cash flows and is also more likely to ensure better utilisation of the vessels as well. Since the main cost goes behind buying the ships and operating them, better utilisation, which is an operational metric, can be considered as a valid financial metric as well.

The table below displays the comparison between Q1 FY24 and Q1 FY25, how the number of fleet has changed and how the utilisation has changed as well.

There has been a marginal reduction in fleet size and the utilisation rate has gone up. Though this may indicate better operational efficiency, it can also mean that there is lesser scope for GNK to take up any new order, specially spot orders. Delving deeper, we can understand what has been the cause of this change.

The figure above shows that compared to Q1 FY24, in Q1 FY25 the usage of Panamax vessel has been removed and the utilisation of the other types of vessel has been stepped up. Panamax is one of the larger vessels whose usage has been reduced.

Based on Q1 FY25, GNK has a greater focus on spot contracts.

  • Capesize spot exposure of ~ 60%
  • Ultramax time-charter exposure of ~65%

This allows GNK to take advantage of more upside potential of prices. However, this naturally comes with the risk of price volatility as well, compared to its peer group like Star Bulk (SBLK) or Golden Ocean (GOGL). The volatility in revenue and earnings may not necessarily be compensated by the upside in earnings in this globally uncertain time.

Headwinds

Strong Performance of FY24 Unlikely to be Sustained

During FY2024, the dry bulk shipping industry witnessed strong performance driven by the Chinese demand for iron and coal and the second was the need for detour in shipping due to the inefficiency in the Suez Canal. The detour will help in increasing the distance that the vessels have to be ferried which increases the earnings for a shipping company like Genco which relies on distance as a metric to monetise its services. Therefore, this acted as a tailwind for Genco to drive its revenue growth as well.

The Chinese demand for iron ore and coal was driven by the need for stockpiling after their construction sector took a major hit. China has been looking to rebuild its inventory levels and 2024 turned out to be the period due to multiple reasons – inventory being depleted after servicing demand for the period post the pandemic; lower commodity prices; upcoming uncertainty regarding the global order and Trump tariffs. All of these factors have led to China's rapidly rising demand for iron ore and coal so that they are able to stockpile it from beforehand that can power their economy for the upcoming period as well. This has led to the supply of shipping also trying to catch up to the demand for shipping services as well. Since heavy industries like shipping work with a lag, the rise in the supply capacity will be beneficial only if the shipping industry is able to witness a similar kind of growth in FY25 as well.

However, I believe that the sudden growth that was seen in FY2024 was a temporary phenomenon, and we are unlikely to face a growth spurt in FY25 as well. The commodity prices may have stabilised, but the global scenario currently faces uncertainty as well. Trump has imposed tariffs as well which is likely to act as a hindrance to global trade flows as well. The KOF Globalisation indicates what has been the level of globalisation and the trend that it has followed.

The graph shows that the level of globalisation has always increased. However, the rate of increase has been gradually decreasing. The slowdown in de facto globalisation has been particularly evident in 2020 onwards as well. Both of these factors combined, we can say that the shipping industry is likely to face certain headwinds as well. This is evident for Genco Shipping as well, which has seen a decrease in revenues from $115.5 million in Q4 FY2023 to $99.2 million in Q4 FY2024.

Tariffs & Fuel Prices

As already discussed above, one of the major headwinds for the shipping industry is likely to be the imposition of tariffs and the subsequent tariff war. This is likely to have a significant impact on the shipping industry as well. Moreover, there have been policies undertaken which indicate the desire of multiple countries to move away from sea-based ferrying. One of the prime examples of this is the India Middle-East Europe Economic Corridor that is illustrated below.

Economically, sea transport is likely to be more beneficial given the high capacity of transportation, lower cost per unit, reduced theft or pillage and reduced carbon footprint as well. However, the reason for moving away from ship transportation is the current heavy dependence on the Suez Canal, which ends up increasing shipping traffic as well, and is also a longer route. Moreover, given the countries that are involved in this agreement, it is one to reckon with for the shipping industry because both India and Saudi Arabia are likely to be the new economic powerhouses. Similarly, China has also decided to set up the Silk Road Economic Belt that is shown below. This will help China to connect to Eurasia and therefore, get access to newer export markets for its goods. Again, there is very little involvement of sea-based ferrying.

In the backdrop of these initiatives, there is the new tariff warfare that has broken out as well. This has and will also lead to a reduction in global trade as well. According to JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), the tariff war could witness a fall in the GDP by as much as 1%. The graph below illustrates the impact on the global GDP and also on some of the key economies as well.

This could go on to translate to shrinking global trade, and thereby lower demand for ferrying services. This comes at a time when the supply of dry bulk ships has been increased, which will decrease the rate of ferrying services further.

However, the impact of tariffs may be limited for GNK because the higher tariffs will likely be passed on by GNK to the end receivers of the goods. Moreover, since only 10% of the revenue of GNK comes from the USA, the impact of retaliatory tariffs is also likely to be limited.

Another factor that has also led to rising costs of operations for shipping companies like Genco is the rising fuel oil prices. The graph below shows the trend historically. The data given below shows us the prices till 2020. As of 2025, the price of Very Low Sulfur Fuel Oil prices are hovering around $580 to $650. Therefore, since 2020, we have seen a steep increase in the ship fuel prices which has led to rising costs of operations, and therefore shrinking margins for shipping companies.

The figure below also illustrates the key shipping routes that GNK follows. It highlights how geopolitical developments can impact the business of shipping businesses.

The routes are mostly condensed around the Indian Ocean region, which has now become a hotbed for geopolitical manoeuvrings of different nations. With increased tensions between world powers like India, China and the USA. We are likely to witness a blockade, especially in the Strait of Malacca, which not only carries 30% of the global trade, but also a major portion of GNK's shipping.

Valuation

The figure below highlights the peer-based valuation. I have deliberately decided not to go ahead with a DCF valuation due to the relatively volatile nature of the cash flow and also the negative cash flows of the company as well. This is a major reason as to why I decided to move ahead with peer-based valuation. The ratios given below provide a mixed picture. Due to the nature of the industry, I would choose to not trust the cash flow based on net profit-based valuation. Therefore, looking at the remaining ratios, it becomes amply clear that among the remaining ratios, we are getting a mostly pessimistic picture for the prospects of Genco Shipping as well. The ratios came from Seeking Alpha.

The figure below also highlights the trend of the financials of Genco as well. The graph makes it amply clear about how much volatility is currently present for Genco Shipping and how this volatility makes it difficult to make a long haul in this company as an investor, in addition to the global headwinds that it is currently facing. This is a major reason as to why I decided to follow mostly sales-based, book-based and operating income based multiples.

Risks

High-Capacity Utilisation

One of the primary risks that could be present is that GNK has one of the best fleet utilisation currently. This implies that GNK is currently not suffering from any overcapacity. The fleet is being utilised well even if we see any potential contraction in shipping demand or slowdown in the growth in demand for shipping it could imply that GNK will still be able to utilise its fleet with a decent level of utilisation rate, and we can still expect significant profitability as well. Similarly, a fall in the fuel prices can also enhance the profitability of GNK as well as that is a significant operating expense that shipping companies have to incur.

Price Chart Indicates Upcoming Recovery

Even though I have delved into the fundamentals of Genco Shipping, retail investors do not always necessarily follow fundamental analysis and often make their buying/selling decisions based on technical analysis. The same technical analysis for Genco Shipping indicates that there could be a risk to the thesis that I have spoken about till now. The figure below shows the chart of the stock price movement.

The figure above highlights that GNK reached an all-time low and is currently on the path to recovery. Therefore, based on my analysis, we are likely to witness a temporary recovery for the stock price of GNK, until the fundamentals catch up. So, unless the management decides to proactively take any action that can turnaround the company, we may soon see a decline in the stock price sooner or later. Based on the recent earnings call, I do not think any such action is currently pending since the management is focusing on doling out dividends, conducting share repurchase programmes, moving towards being net debt, etc.

Conclusion

So, based on the trends that are currently being seen in the shipping industry and the global trends as well, I personally believe that the shipping industry is likely to be in for a rough time. Even the positive push it received from Chinese demand and also the Suez Canal blockage, may only have been a temporary relief as well. The valuation also reveals a similar insight about how there may be excessive optimism regarding a shipping company like Genco Shipping as well, along with the relatively volatile financials that add to the risk of holding to it as well. Having said that, there are also certain risks to my thesis that need to be acknowledged as well. Based on the comprehensive analysis, I would provide a recommendation of HOLD for this particular stock.

data.title: Hims & Hers: Growth, Strategic Partnerships, And Operational Leverage (Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

There have been some developments with Hims & Hers Health (NYSE:HIMS) since my last evaluation in February. Hims is a rapidly growing company. For context, in 2020, Hims had only 181 employees. This number has ballooned to 1,637 in 2024. In the same vein, revenues grew from $148.8 million to $1.476 billion in the same period.

My February analysis followed Hims’ third quarter 2024 earnings. It was growing revenues at 77% year-over-year. There seemed to be some acceleration at this point, as well. In the past, SG&A expenses made up over 80% of revenues. This dropped to 68% in the third quarter. It is important to watch this trend because it’s a key hindrance to its bottom line. This is the nature of Hims’ business. It costs a lot of money to acquire customers. Then the challenge is keeping customers.

Notably, much of Hims’ growth was associated with momentum in its obesity segment, thanks to semaglutide compounds. Unfortunately for Hims, it was limited to manufacturing semaglutide compounds during the brand drug’s shortage, which ended some months after it began. To maintain momentum in its obesity segment, the strategy (back then) was to launch a generic version of liraglutide, another GLP-1 that is inferior to semaglutide.

My discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis revealed that Hims was priced for about 19% revenue growth, which didn’t seem unreasonable despite Hims appearing overvalued on a price/free cash flow basis.

Despite Hims’ strong revenue growth and brand strength, I ended with a cautious Hold rating due to its heavy reliance on aggressive marketing and the FDA’s February declaration that the shortage was over.

The stock slumped for weeks following my analysis, but has since climbed back. Overall, HIMS is up about 20%.

The following article revisits the telehealth company following its first quarter earnings report and recent developments.

Recent Developments

I am most interested in how efficiently and sustainably tech companies grow. Looking back at Hims’ financial performance over the past five years (now that we have full 2024 data), it scores an “High” on my Growth Quality Dashboard, which evaluates seven critical dimensions.

Naturally, the market likes to focus on gross margins and revenue growth. There are some concerning trends on the former. In the first quarter of 2025, Hims’ gross margin was just 73% compared to 82% in the same period last year. This is attributed to Hims’ weight loss segment. While heavy in volume, these are lower-margin products. Hims does anticipate some improvement in gross margins beginning in the second quarter. Assuming this is the case, Hims’ unit economics are still trending in the right direction.

Revenue of $586 million in the first quarter represented 111% year-over-year growth. Hims beat its consensus revenue estimate by $47 million in the first quarter. Notably, SG&A expenses were just 58% of revenues (aka "sales efficiency") in the first quarter. Cash from operations and free cash flow were $109.1 million and $50.1 million, respectively. So, Hims is continuing to make gains in cash generation.

Critically, its subscriber count reached nearly 2.4 million by the end of the first quarter of 2025, representing a 38% increase year-over-year. Hims is also squeezing more from average subscribers, growing 53% year-over-year to reach $84/month.

While Hims added liraglutide earlier this year, the big news was the collaboration between Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Hims in April. Consumers can now purchase Wegovy (semaglutide) through Hims’ platform. So, Wegovy figures to drive people to its platform. On the other hand, this is unlikely to be a high-margin venture, and it introduces dependency on Novo.

Looking ahead to the second quarter, Hims’ guidance of revenue in the range of $530 million to $550 million was viewed as “soft,” given analyst expectations of around $565 million.

As for new offerings, Hims intends to launch low testosterone and menopause support before the end of the year. This is a natural extension of its services focused on “sensitive issues.” In February, Hims acquired an at-home lab testing company. This will enable its clients to perform blood draws at home.

Despite declining R&D intensity, Hims is active in MedMatch AI, which looks to personalize treatment and improve health outcomes. This is Hims’ moat amid extreme competition in telehealth, right? Hims’ emphasis on personalization.

Hims’ $870 million convertible senior notes offering signals its intention to expand. This came shortly after ambitious 2030 financial targets of $6.5 billion in revenue and $1.3 billion in EBITDA. This should get Hims’ cash and short-term investments ($322.7 million as of March 2025) well over $1 billion.

Valuation Assumptions

As for inputs, $2.34 billion in revenue (59% year-over-year growth) represents the consensus analyst estimate for 2025 (“Year 1”). 25% revenue growth and 20% FCF margin are averages throughout the nine-year projection period. They are also feasible based on historical trends and the telehealth market (which is projected to grow at a 24.7% CAGR). HIMS beta of 1.85 represents a 5Y Monthly. Stock beta differs by source and timeframe, but I thought this was most appropriate. Everything else is standard DCF model methodology.

Again, my goal here is not to determine whether a stock is overvalued (markets are efficient most of the time), but to uncover the expectations regarding growth and margins implied by HIMS’ market valuation.

Judging by the "Year 6" (2030) projection of $7.14 billion, Hims expects a revenue CAGR closer to 20% in the next few years.

My sensitivity analysis shows that HIMS is priced for 23.1% revenue growth, assuming all other things are the same.

The company likely has some flexibility on margins. For instance, if Hims can achieve 25% FCF margins, growth expectations could fall slightly <20% and still support its valuation. On the other hand, should margin expectations fall under 20%, growth would have to boost to 25%.

My Monte Carlo simulation accounts for changes to key DCF inputs like growth, beta, and risk-free rate. The outcome is more dynamic and realistic than the DCF model.

This tells us that there is some wiggle room in HIMS' valuation. In other words, the stock could rally (or "random walk") to $90.30 before becoming grossly overvalued (assuming no major changes to key assumptions). In any event, any small shifts in assumptions can radically alter HIMS’ stock price. This is no surprise given its beta. This is not a stock for the faint of heart.

Conclusion

To wrap things up, things are going quite well at Hims & Hers. The brand is stronger than ever, growth is tantalizing, and the company’s cash generation is accelerating. The recent capital infusion will help support growth initiatives in its expanding portfolio. Still, there are some risks to consider. Customer acquisition costs remain high, gross margins are lower from new product categories, and Hims’ dependence on GLP-1 market stability is increasing. Externally, the company faces threats from increasing competition (e.g., Ro, Amazon, Teladoc), price competition, and macroeconomic headwinds that could reduce discretionary health spending.

All in all, HIMS’s financial progress, market share growth, and operating leverage make it a Buy (rating upgrade), particularly suited for a barbell portfolio. HIMS is a battleground stock, so be prepared for continued volatility and allocate funds accordingly.

data.title: eToro: A Successful Debut For This Crypto/Broker Play
data.description:
Shares of eToro Group (NASDAQ:ETOR) have seen a strong public debut, as the online broker saw shares rise by nearly 30% to $67 per share on the first day of trading.

While growth and excitement around crypto might drive these returns, I have real concerns about the business model after a look under the hood. While this might have to do with my own conservative thoughts, I am happy to pass on this opportunity.

Trading Platform

eToro has a vision to open global markets for everyone to trade and invest in both a simple and transparent way. The company was founded back in 2007 on the back of this idea, with markets at the time still dominated by traditional financial institutions which focused on investors with a substantial capital base.

Besides simplicity, the company has been focuses on the power of technology, fostering a culture of sharing, innovation and adoption of cryptocurrencies in its offerings.

With the passage of time, the company has grown to a base of 3.5 million funded accounts as of year-end 2024, with customers located across 75 countries. The company has been benefiting from the rise of popularity of (crypto) investing and the focus on younger age cohorts which appreciate the learning and collaboration services offered as well.

These users have a total of $16.6 billion of assets under administration with average balances coming in at $47k, with typical trades averaging in the hundreds of dollars, both applying to equity trading and crypto trading.

Valuation and IPO Thoughts

eToro initially aimed to sell 10 million shares at a preliminary price range between $46 and $50 per share, with the share sale split evenly between shares offered by the company and those offered by selling shareholders.

Strong demand made that the offer was priced at $52 per share, with the offering upsized to 12 million shares being sold. With nearly 83 million shares outstanding post the public offering, equity of the business was valued at $4.3 billion at the offer levels. Given the rise to $67 per share on the first day of trading, this market value has risen by over a billion to $5.6 billion.

Pro forma cash and equivalents stand at $878 million with liabilities reported at $358 million, for a net cash position of roughly half a billion, implying that operating assets are valued at around $5 billion.

If we look at the results, we see the complications in the business model. Total revenues were reported at $6.3 billion, $3.9 billion and $12.6 billion, respectively in the year 2022/2023 and 2024. The vast majority of these sales (typically around 90%, or more) has been derived from revenue from crypto assets, with the company reporting trading profits and losses on this category as well.

On the bottom line, an operating loss of $215 million was reported in 2022, followed by a $28 million gain in 2023 and $245 million gain in 2024. While these are large numbers, they are tiny in relation to revenues, but moreover highly volatile and reliant on crypto. Moreover, it is not just reliance on crypto volumes, but also pricing risks, which differentiate eToro from other brokers (as the company also reports inventories on the balance sheet) which is not appealing in my view, to put it mildly.

Looking at the preliminary first quarter results for 2025, the company sees the number of accounts rise to nearly 3.6 million, yet adjusted EBITDA for the quarter is seen down slightly from the first quarter of 2024, which was relatively strong in all fairness. In either case, results are very volatile and hard to read into, with volatility largely used by net revenues from crypto assets, complemented by more stable trading income from and equity and other asset classes, net interest income and currency conversion & other income.

Concluding Thoughts

I have real concerns and questions on the IPO of eToro and its business. Results, including sales and earnings, are very volatile and comprised largely out of net crypto revenues, which are not just very volatile but involve principal risk to the business as well (unlike the true definition of a broker).

Given this, there are many risks, including volatility in prices and activity levels, stiff competition, real risks which not only include regular business risks, but also that of cybersecurity, theft, reputation risks, compliance, and others.

With earnings power looking to be relatively limited in relation to the current valuation, and me having real concerns on the business and sustainability of the business model, I am erring cautious here.

As a result, I refrain from investing, more driven by concerns on business (model) rather than the valuation (which looks high as well) although that my conservative thought could easily be proven wrong. After all, somewhat related and similar platforms like Robin Hood (HOOD) and Coinbase (COIN) have seen success as well.

Given all this, I am erring on the cautious side, not having an interest in getting here, despite and inspire the very successful public debut made by the business.

data.title: Mainstreet Equity: A Quality Compounder Hiding In Plain Sight
data.description:
Mainstreet Equity (TSX:MEQ:CA) has delivered exceptional returns for investors using a simple, repeatable formula—which has delivered tremendous growth with very minimal share dilution. This formula can easily be repeated, no matter where the company expands, meaning this growth story still has loads of long-term potential.

(Mainstreet Equity trades on the TSX and reports in CAD. All figures are in CAD unless indicated otherwise).

Introduction

Led by founder and CEO Bob Dhillon, Mainstreet Equity debuted on the Toronto Stock Exchange in 2000. It owned 272 apartment units with a market value of $17M.

Unlike most other publicly traded residential landlords who pay out a large portion of their earning back to shareholders, Mainstreet embraced a different strategy. The company poured its earnings back into the company, using its profits to acquire additional apartments.

Mainstreet has kept this up for 25 years now, and growth has been spectacular. The company surpassed $1B in assets in 2012 and $2B in assets by 2019. It recently surpassed $3B in assets in 2024.

That works out to a 16% CAGR since the IPO, which is a wonderful long-term growth rate.

After getting its start in Alberta and expanding aggressively throughout the province in the 2000s and 2010s, Mainstreet has diversified outside of its home province in the past decade. As it stands today, Mainstreet owns 18,451 apartment units. 10,157 (or about 54% of units) are located in Alberta, mostly in Calgary and Edmonton—although the company has expanded into smaller communities in the province. It owns 4,255 units in neighboring British Columbia, and an additional 3,634 units in Saskatchewan. It has also begun expanding into Manitoba, where it owns 405 units.

British Columbia features more expensive real estate than Canada's prairie provinces, so even though the portfolio there is only 32% of total units, it accounts for 43% of net asset value (NAV).

Mainstreet has followed a pretty simple business plan throughout its history as a publicly traded company. It's a six-step process that looks quite simple on the surface, but is deceptively effective.

  1. The company identifies and buys underperforming mid-market apartment units at well below replacement costs. It focuses on smaller buildings without amenities like elevators or parking garages, since those tend to be expensive to maintain.
  2. It then renovates its new asset, using similar finishes in all of its units. That allows the company to buy materials like flooring, light fixtures, and appliances in bulk, which leads to cost savings versus other landlords. It'll also make improvements to the outside of the building.
  3. Mainstreet then focuses on lowering operational costs. This includes installing energy saving equipment, using its own property managers, and implementing technology to help keep costs down.
  4. Once the first three steps are done, Mainstreet repositions the property in the market and increases rents. It uses the Mainstreet brand to help attract tenants, and professional property managers give a higher level of service compared to many peers. Many competing properties are owned by landlords who have full-time jobs and other responsibilities that keep them away.
  5. The property is then refinanced, pulling out capital to pay for the next renovation. The most successful deals will result in zero company dollars invested in the property.
  6. Occasionally the company will sell mature real estate for a large premium versus its own internal valuation. But this is relatively rate; it's primarily a buy and hold operator.

Here's the business model in a nutshell:

It translates into a portfolio that costs the company approximately $125,000 per door, and earns average rent of $1,200 per unit on a monthly basis. New buildings in Canada, meanwhile, cost about $400,000 per door and rent for about $3,000 per unit each month. That translates into an 11.5% rental yield for Mainstreet versus about a 9% rental yield for investors who build new units.

Mainstreet's units are also more affordable, which is helpful to the large chunk of Canadians who don't make huge incomes.

For those of us who live in Western Canada, it seems like Mainstreet is everywhere. It has grown significantly over the last 25 years without expanding past Canada's four westernmost provinces.

This has caused some to question the company's growth potential going forward. They believe Mainstreet is running out of apartments to acquire, and expanding to other provinces just isn't in the cards.

I believe such a conclusion is premature. Let's take a closer look at the company's growth potential to see why.

Growth Potential

Mainstreet has three growth paths that it can pursue. First I'll talk about the potential remaining in its current growth path in Western Canada, then I'll expand on the opportunity to expand its business model to the eastern part of the country. Finally, I'll talk a little bit about the potential to expand the business model to the United States.

Let's begin with the current operations in Western Canada. Mainstreet owns more than 18,000 apartments in Canada's four westernmost provinces. Just how much potential does it have to acquire additional units in its current markets?

It turns out Mainstreet still has ample growth potential. It has barely scratched the surface.

According to company research, there are 237,466 mid-market apartments in the metros where it currently operates in Western Canada, and a total of more than 339,000 total rental units. Mainstreet focuses on properties with less than 100 units for the reasons I mentioned before.

It owns approximately 8% of the market today. That's not much.

We'll also note that Mainstreet's target market excludes Vancouver, the largest city in Western Canada. Include it, and the target market suddenly becomes larger.

Mainstreet's dominance in the west also comes with one distinct advantage. Many apartments in the region are owned by baby boomer investors who purchased their properties in the 1980s, 1990s, or 2000s. They've collected steadily increasing rent for decades, and even making improvements will see significant gains when it's time to sell.

Knowing how big Mainstreet is in the region, many of these investors make Mainstreet their first call when it's time to sell. The company has a steady stream of sellers that come to them, which is helpful when it comes to sourcing deals.

Mainstreet also continues to acquire in Western Canada. So far, in 2025 it has purchased:

  • 25 units in Abbotsford, B.C. for $4.475M
  • 43 units in Calgary, Alberta for $7.8M
  • 48 units in Prince George, B.C. for $5.5M
  • 182 units in Red Deer, Alberta for $15.5M
  • A 4,500 square foot commercial building next to its office in Edmonton for $960,000

And that's on top of the 1,296 units the company purchased in 2024.

These consistent acquisitions also create a steady pipeline of growth opportunities. Essentially, Mainstreet is almost always working on bringing properties back to market. This pipeline ensures earnings growth is still strong as renovations are completed and properties are leased again.

Next, let's talk about expanding the company's business model to Eastern Canada. There's significant potential there simply from a population perspective. Ontario and Quebec have a combined population of approximately 24M, compared to approximately 12M people for the four provinces Mainstreet operates in today.

Both provinces also have a bunch of older, mid-market apartments that could potentially be a fit for Mainstreet's business model.

Landlord tenant laws are quite similar throughout Canada. The only difference between Ontario and Quebec versus Alberta and Saskatchewan is the former have various forms of provincial rent controls. Mainstreet would be limited in how much it can increase rents.

That would be an issue when expanding into Eastern Canada, but not a huge one. Mainstreet has already expanded into British Columbia and Manitoba, two provinces that already have rent control laws in place.

The bigger issue seems likely to be property prices, especially in the largest cities. Toronto is one of the most unaffordable real estate markets on the planet, and it seems unlikely Mainstreet can find anything to buy there that would meet its investment criteria. Its suburbs aren't much better. Montreal may prove to be a more fertile hunting ground.

Still, even without Toronto and Montreal, smaller cities in both provinces should offer plenty of opportunity.

Finally, I'll quickly mention the company's potential to expand into the United States. The U.S. Midwest has a bunch of similarities to Canada's prairie provinces, including many mid-market apartments, similar income levels, landlord friendly rental laws, and relatively affordable real estate. I believe the potential to expand into that part of the United States is an attractive one over the long-term, but not something the company would have to do in the next decade. It still has loads of potential inside Canada before expanding into the United States.

Insider Ownership

One of the more attractive parts of this growth story is that Mainstreet has expanded so much while keeping its share count under control.

The company debuted on the Toronto Stock Exchange in 2000 with 8.883M shares. It has periodically repurchased stock over the years and then issued stock options. It also did an equity raise at one point to help pay for an acquisition.

Yet, the number of shares outstanding is only up less than 10% over the last 25 years, increasing to 9.318M.

Most of Mainstreet's competitors are REITs, which are serial issuers of shares to help fund growth. Its impressive results versus competitors on the share dilution front is quite impressive.

The CEO is also a major shareholder. As of the end of 2024, Bob Dhillon owned more than 4.3M shares, or 46.3% of the company.

Valuation

Mainstreet has delivered impressive growth over the last 24 years. From 2000 through the end of 2024, the company grew revenue by 16% per year and FFO per share by 18% per year. FFO per share increased from $0.18 per share in 2000 to $9.09 per share in 2024.

Despite posting such excellent growth—and having long-term potential to continue expanding the business at a fairly high rate—shares are still relatively cheap from a price-to-FFO perspective. Especially if we look towards the future.

As I write this, Mainstreet shares trade hands for $191.50 each. That translates into a trailing price-to-FFO ratio of 21x.

The stock gets cheaper when we shift our outlook forward. The company isn't widely covered by analysts, but the few who do follow it estimate that FFO will increase by more than 19% in 2025, reaching $10.82 per share. Similar growth is expected for 2026, with FFO per share increasing a further 15% to reach $12.50 per unit.

That puts shares at under 18x 2025's estimated FFO, and then 15.4x 2026's estimated FFO. That's a very reasonable price for a company that looks poised to continue growing by 15%+ on an annual basis.

Risks

One big risk that could derail the company's growth, the price of crude oil. Much of Mainstreet's portfolio is located in areas of Western Canada with high oil production. If oil falls, and it drags down local economies with it, both occupancy and rents would likely follow.

The other risk is something that could impact just about any growth stock. There's no guarantee the company's expansion plans could happen as planned. Sellers may no longer be willing to sell, or deals might not be available at attractive rates of returns.

The Bottom Line

Mainstreet has delivered impressive growth in its almost 25-year history as a publicly traded company, but most investors simply aren't paying attention.

It has executed its growth plan almost flawlessly. But despite its success, this analyst still believes there's plenty of potential left. It owns just 8% of apartments in its target markets, and after that, there is loads of potential to take the same business plan and execute it in other parts of North America.

Shares are a little expensive if you look backwards, but become quite reasonably valued when we look forward. The unique business model allows the company to keep the growth going via acquisitions and then pull most of the capital back out when new assets are renovated and put back into the rental pool.

Add in other goodies like the CEO's large ownership stake and the lack of dilution over the years, and I'm a fan. I rate shares a buy, and would be aggressively adding to my position on a prolonged pullback.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: European Rapeseed Oil Futures Enter The Market On Strong Biofuels Demand
data.description:
By Paul Wightman

At a Glance

  • While some hydrotreated vegetable oils or renewable diesel are produced from rapeseed oil, biofuels remain a key source of demand
  • The launch of European Rapeseed Oil futures marks a significant transformation for a market that has traditionally relied on liquidity in a physical forward market

The European rapeseed oil market is one of the largest vegetable oil markets in the world, playing a crucial role in food, feed and bioenergy.

Vegetable oil-based derivatives volumes have been rising, in part due to the growing importance of bioenergy markets such as biomass-based diesel and ethanol used in transportation, as well as biogas used for heating and electricity generation.

In this sphere, the biggest futures and options market is for soybean oil, which is traded globally as one of the leading benchmarks for the vegetable oil sector. However, European rapeseed oil has been gaining ground amid rising demand for road transport biodiesel, with demand particularly high during the colder months due to its superior performance compared to other feedstocks.

The growing embrace of bioenergy has seen demand rise across the vegetable oil sector and has been beneficial to products like Soybean Oil, Palm Oil and Used Cooking Oil futures, a trend that looks set to continue – vegetable oil demand for sustainable aviation fuels remains relatively strong according to data provider Argus Media, and total vegetable oil-based futures volume in the 12 months to March 2025 reached 4.254 million contracts per month, up 19% from 3.58 million contracts per month in the prior 12-month period.

Rising Volatility in European Rapeseed Oil

Hedging against the price volatility in the rapeseed oil market has become an increasingly common trade in recent years. The chart below shows that the price of rapeseed oil has been rising for the past 15–18 months, which can be attributed to the demand for biofuels. While volatility appears to be falling from levels seen in the early part of 2023, the 30-day historical annualized volatility has traded between 14% and 21% since the final quarter of 2023 as more firms enter the market to hedge against the underlying commodity cost.

With the addition of European Rapeseed Oil futures to CME Group’s suite of vegetable oil-based derivatives contracts, market participants now have a tool that allows them to hedge against market volatility, especially during a time when shifting trade policies are reshaping the global flow of commodities.

Rising Demand for Biofuels in Europe

Among European biofuels, rapeseed oil remains the dominant feedstock, accounting for around 50% of the total biomass-based diesel in 2024. Strong demand for rapeseed oil comes partly from demand for biofuels such as rapeseed oil methyl ester, one of the leading biofuel markets in Europe.

A range of feedstocks are used in the biofuels sector, and advanced feedstocks such as used cooking oil (UCO) have gained ground against traditional crop-based biofuels in recent years due to their higher greenhouse gas-savings credentials.

However, concerns persist about shortages in the sector, in part due to the continued volatility in the supply chains from countries like China, a significant global exporter of UCO. The volatile supply chains are largely caused by a mismatch between demand and available supply combined with some quality issues for European buyers, though quality issues are being addressed by more comprehensive regulation as the trade flows continue to develop. The volume of rapeseed oil being used in the biofuels sector is around 6.5-million tons based on latest data for 2024.

Rapeseed - A Dual Purpose Feedstock

In the biofuels sector, traders closely monitor the relationship between rapeseed oil and biodiesel fuel Rapeseed Methyl Ester (RME) as this partly determines the production margin for biodiesel producers.

The spread between the cash price of Argus RME and Argus rapeseed oil has been weakening since the fourth quarter of 2023, in part due to the relatively strong demand for rapeseed oil as a feedstock for energy markets coupled with its use as an animal feed. Since the final quarter of 2023 the spread between the cash price of RME and rapeseed oil has weakened from around €400 to just below €100 per metric ton in mid-April 2025, according to data from Argus Media.

As risks persist in the market, European Rapeseed Oil futures offer market participants a way to manage this uncertainty and are a valuable addition to the well-established futures and options markets for U.S. Soybean Oil and Malaysian Palm Oil.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
